title: Myroxylon schaefferioides
intro: Myroxylon schaefferioides, also known as Palo Santo or Bursera Schaefferioides, is a small tree or shrub native to Central and South America, belonging to the family Burseraceae. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal and aromatic properties, offering health benefits such as pain relief, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial effects. The bioactive constituents of Myroxylon schaefferioides include sesquiterpenes, diterpenes, and essential oils, including safrole and myroxiron. The most common herbal preparations made with this plant are essential oils, extracts, and resin, which are often used in aromatherapy, perfumery, and traditional medicine.
taxonomy: Myroxylon schaefferioides is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Fabaceae, commonly known as the pea or legume family. It is a member of the genus Myroxylon, which consists of several species of trees native to Central and South America. The plant's classification is further broken down into the kingdom Plantae, phylum Angiospermophyta, and class Magnoliopsida. Myroxylon schaefferioides is also known by its synonyms, including Myroxylon tomentosum and Myroxylon tomentosum var. schaefferioides. The plant's classification reflects its unique characteristics and evolutionary history within the plant kingdom.
